comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's nearly midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. (I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The picture?  I'm working my way through photos of my lineage, focusing on the teachers whose work has influenced me and the teachers who influenced them in turn. I'm currently tracing my Martinist lineage, and at this point we're getting deep into strange territory. Baron Karl Gotthelf von Hund was a German freemason, born in 1722, and raised a Master Mason in 1742. In 1743, while in Paris, he received initiation into a secret set of high Masonic degrees presided over by a mysterious personage, the Knight of the Red Feather, whose identity he was pledged never to reveal. He was then appointed the head of the secret degrees in Germany, and proceeded to found the Rite of Strict Observance, conferring its degrees on a great many Masons from Germany and elsewhere, including Jean-Baptiste Willermoz, who was honored here a couple of weeks ago. He then tried to contact the Secret Chiefs from whom he'd received his initiation...and got dead silence. He continued to run the Strict Observance until his death in 1776. 

Afterwards, the absence of any further word from the Secret Chiefs led members of the Strict Observance to call a general meeting in 1782 in Wilhelmsbad, where they voted to set aside Hund's claims and embrace instead a new ritual that had been written by Jean-Baptiste Willermoz. Willermoz's Rectified Rite had a complicated history thereafter, but it remains a living tradition in some Masonic jurisdictions and also in the Martinist movement. As for the Knight of the Red Feather -- well, that's a story for another week...

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: Review on The Book of the New Sun, Sunne by Wolcensmen, and Question on Singing
comments.content:
Good Evening,

I hope everyone here is well, and that the waxing moon is helping you start and build what you want to see more of in your life.

To Share: Two things to share this week: 1. I just wrapped up reading Gene Wolfe's The Book of the New Sun and its sequel The Urth of the New Sun. I found them excellent, but a bit hard to easily describe. Still, I gave it my best shot in a short review (of especial interest here: the books make a lot of use of references to/symbolism from the Tarot, Cabala, esoteric Christianity, myth, and lots else): https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_thoughts_on_the_book_of_the_new_sun
2. I've been enjoying the music of English "Dark Folk" band Wolcensmen for a while now, and I'm surprised I haven't recommended them yet. I like pretty much everything they've put out, but for any Heathens here, I've taken to using their song "Sunne" as a greeting to the Sun most every morning (some here may recognize that song as the theme to Survive the Jive videos).

To Ask: Thanks to 2. above, I've been thinking about other ways to bring singing into my worship and/or magic. From JMG, or anyone else, I'd appreciate any pointers to resources mundane, religious, or esoteric on singing, especially for religious or magical purposes (I can carry a familiar tune alrigh, but I'm not a trained singer at all, if that helps).

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

To any who will have them, I put forth my blessings and best wishes,
Jeff

Keep in mind that when you first start vibrating a sound, it is going to sound cartoonish, overly nasal, and funny, like something from Spongebob or Looney Toons. replies.content:
On 2: I'd simply add a caution. There are a lot of religious music traditions out there. They're all different, and they've all developed for specific purposes. Learning and using music entrains you in very deep and interesting ways... so it is absolutely worth doing, but at the same time, if you don't already have a music tradition that's been handed down to you, I'm not at all sure you could play mix-and-match, or borrow from other traditions... unless you want to be shaped in the way the music from those parent traditions are intended to shape people.

If you're starting from scratch, you may just have to try stuff out, and then ask: what is this doing to me, and is this what I wanted/needed it to do? What does this move me toward?

comments.content:
Dear JMG and Commenters,

I hope you are well and healthy.

Some random questions, about subjects that came through here and made me think about them:

1. It is correct to associate:
- the material and etheric plane with Malkuth;
- astral to yesod;
- mental to netzach, hod and tiphereth; - causal to Geburah and Hesed; and
- Divine to Binah, Hochmah and Kether?

2. Can we think of a traditional North American occultism, which developed apart from European esotericism, notably English and French, with its own concepts and ideas? Can we also point out some early works and more important developers?

3. Two weeks ago we talked about repeating family patterns. Some are very visible and appear mainly in the professional paths we choose. Other patterns, however, should be harder to notice. My question is, is there anything magical I can do to break these patterns?

4. For Catholics and Christians here: what's the best way to work with the archangel Gavriel to achieve healing from an illness?

Thank you very much for the opportunity and have a good week!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) There are various ways to associate the spheres and the planes. I prefer to associate Malkuth with the material plane, Yesod with the etheric plane, Hod and Netzach with the astral plane, Tiphareth with the mental plane, Geburah and Chesed with the spiritual plane, Binah and Chokmah with the causal plane, and Kether with the divine plane.

2) Yes, very much so. The American tradition in occultism has roots in German and West African occultism as well as English and French; among its significant early figures are Andrew Jackson Davis, Phineas Quimby, and Paschal Beverly Randolph; among its later figures of importance are George Winslow Plummer and the incomparable Manly Palmer Hall.

3) You need to understand them, become conscious of them, and choose not to express them. That can be pursued in various ways, though meditation is generally more useful than magic.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: 2) Gow interesting! I wouldn't have guessed this Germanic influence on traditional American occultism. how was this bridge made? Is there also any influence from Native American spirituality?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There was a huge amount of German immigration to the American colonies -- so much that the Continental Congress seriously considered making German rather than English the official language of the new United States. Many of the people who came over from Germany were religious minority groups fleeing religious persecution -- that's how we got the Amish, the Mennonites, the Moravian Brethren, and a number of Rosicrucian groups. Many of them practiced some form of magic, and their magic became widespread in early America; the first magical manual written in this country, The Long Lost Friend by Johann Georg Hohmann, was written in German and published by a Pennsylvania "Dutch" (i.e., Deutsch) publisher in 1820.

As for Native American magic, no, as far as I know very little if any of that got picked up, because of the long and bitter hostilities between the native peoples and the European settlers.

replies.content:
I know effectively nothing about German occultism, but to JMG's point, Harper's recently ran a piece from a writer searching for the occult roots of her Amish heritage, if it's of any interest: https://harpers.org/archive/2023/07/in-the-glimmer-good-witches-of-pennsylvania

Axé,
Fra' Lupo

comments.title: Helping out a friend
comments.content:
A friend of mine is in a tight spot. She’s trapped by poverty in a household of Covidian cultists the quality of whose behavior has declined noticeably over the past three years. A member of the household more secure in his position therein than herself is a small-time pusher. This man has been persecuting her in various ways, harassing and stalking her. Materially, she has no way of defending herself that I know of. No one in the house will stand up for her, and if she raises too much of a complaint she could wind up out in the street. She’s not young and jobs are not growing on trees these days, so this could lead to a really grim situation.

Dealing with some pretty scary troubles of my own during the past eighteen months, I’ve found the teachings of Neville Goddard very helpful. I’ve tried to suggest to her using some of these. But I don’t seem to be the master teacher of spiritual insight that Mr. Goddard was. She evidently thinks I’m just giving her New Age pink bubble happy talk, and I don’t think she’s tried seriously to apply any of my suggestions.

I know from experience that when you are being actively persecuted by some tormentor it’s damn near impossible to focus on much of anything but what they are doing to you and the negative emotional state which that causes in you, and clearly this is happening with her.

I’ve come to dread her phone calls. I don’t have any solutions other than what I’ve already offered. So she just lays her troubles on me, exhaustingly.

I have some background in HOGD. All I can think of is to perform the Rose Cross ritual for her, repeatedly - probably nightly, for some time. More than once she’s given me permission to do this. So I think this would be all right.

You’ve said before that magic is a not a cure-all, and this may be one of those times when its utility is very limited. It would be helpful to know what is your perspective on this.

replies.title: Re: Helping out a friend
replies.content:
This sounds uncomfortably like the classic Rescue Game: your friend has assigned herself the status of Victim, the other person in the household is the Persecutor, and you are the Rescuer, except that (as usual in this form of the game) you're not allowed to actually Rescue her, just to serve as her audience. You can tell that it's this kind of Rescue Game when no attempt to change the situation is acceptable to the Victim.

There is no endpoint to the Rescue Game, just endless rounds of her dumping her emotions on you and refusing to do anything to change the situation. If you're comfortable with that, by all means continue. If not, you will have to quit the game, because she won't.

(You might find it helpful to read a copy of Eric Berne's Games People Play, which gives the psychological background to the above remarks.)

replies.title: Re: Helping out a friend
replies.content:
Thanks for your input. Yes, I recall your major post and extended discussions on the Rescue Game from some years back, and now that you bring it to my mind in this context the pattern does seem to fit.

Moreover the emotional dumping you mention not only is real but leaves me feeling drained, as though some kind of emotional vampirism is being practiced. The fact that I made repeated attempts to help as best I could and she did nothing, repeatedly, with the information I gave her tells its own tale.

I don’t like to seem like a false or shallow friend, but I don’t feel like there’s anything I can do to improve her situation if she won’t. I think I’ll have to stop answering her calls, and make none myself. And to speak truth, this gives me a huge sense of relief.

I really appreciate your remarks. I would otherwise have felt heartless taking this line of action (or pointed inaction), even though I had a pretty strong sense that something was off. Now I see it simply as a matter of self-protection. It’s not like I don’t have troubles enough of my own to keep me occupied.

comments.title: Temporary Suspension of Magical Practice
comments.content:
For a few years now I have had a regular routine of daily LBRP and Middle Pillar, weekly Rose Cross, and some other things. I have found all of this to be tremendously beneficial, and plan to continue it for the foreseeable future.

In a few months, I plan to go on a long pilgrimage. It will take 4-6 weeks, during which time the basic plan is to walk for ten or twelve hours and give prayer and offering at two or three temples each day. Lodging will mostly be in close quarters with other pilgrims, or out of doors. I don't expect to have space, let alone privacy, in which to perform my daily rituals.

On the one hand, I can think of arguments in favor of continuing the practice through difficulties. On the other hand, since I will already be setting aside work, family, and practically every other aspect of daily life, I think it might be better to just focus on the experience of the pilgrimage. I really don't want to interrupt my ritual practice, but I think it might be appropriate in this circumstance.

Of course I will be praying and meditating on this. If JMG or anyone in the commentariat have some relevant experience, I would be interested to hear your perspectives.

comments.title: Someone teaching a magical course for $100 a month for a course of seven months
comments.content:
While trudging steadily along "Learning Ritual Magic" I also occasionally come across other material and authors that have their own takes on all things magical. Recently I have been recommended to take a look into one Jason Miller, who has, if I understand correctly, a sizable following.

Now he writes books, but also offers a course for which he charges $100 a month for the duration of seven months. He advertises his course with these words:

There is more to Magic than begging the Gods for favors.
YOU are the Witch directing the power according to YOUR agency.


Reading this made me quite startled, actually. I see several dangers. First this seems like a perfect opportunity to really mess things up. Who in their right mind would like to gain "power" that is not in alignment with gods? Would that not just exaggarate the imbalances that are already there? It is almost as if Miller is purposefully appealing to the base needs of a wide audience in order to cash in a pretty penny.

Also, of course, participants are sworn to secrecy, so the material does not spread, nor can there be meaningful public critique. And after spending $700, you kind of have to become a true believer or otherwise you need to face the cognitive dissonance of your choices.

1) Are you familiar with Miller?
2) If so, what is your take on him?
3) My current take is that since there are these red flags already, there is a high chance that his books are also somewhat tainted - even if their methods work. Would that seem like a reasonable assumption?

replies.title: Re: Someone teaching a magical course for $100 a month for a course of seven months
replies.content:
Miller went off the rails badly during the Trump Presidency. He was advocating for and posting curses against Trump's administration. Someone pointed out back then that Miller admitted to working with demons.

All that said, there are supposedly sane methods of using one's own power for magical work, though they either are weak or use etheric (life force) energy. (At least the one's I know of.)

replies.title: Re: Someone teaching a magical course for $100 a month for a course of seven months
replies.content:
If I may, I feel like there is some context that is necessary to understand that quote. Note the words "there is more to." As in, he is saying that magic includes practices other than just asking for things.

Many of the people who tried pop-occult psychological methods found them ineffective, but later discovered petitions. Petitioning is literally what it sounds like--making an altar to a particular being, making offerings to them, and asking them for a favor. It many people's experience, it gets better results than the "psychological" approach. As a result, there are a fair number of people who literally believe in the existence of spirits and have experience making petitions, but who feel like "magic" includes more possibilities than just asking for goodies, and who aren't really sure how to move forward. That's the demographic Jason Miller is marketing to--not people who have never tried anything in magic, not people who aren't really sure if they believe in all that occult stuff, and not people who are currently happy with the results they're getting from their ceremonial practice. I'm sure he'd be happy if those people bought his books and courses, as he is indeed a pretty focused businessman, but his primary demographic is pretty specific.

As for working with divine powers, note that he offers a course focused on working with the goddess Hekate (probably where that quote came from), a course focused on working with Saint Cyprian, and a course focused on working with the god Jupiter. His approach is definitely "works with" rather than "religiously worships," and I'm more religious personally, but everything I've seen is definitely still collaborative and respectful.

As for more spiritual aims, yes he teaches and focuses primarily or practical magic without a major focus on spiritual development--many traditions and teachers do. He calls himself a sorcerer for that reason and makes the goals pretty clear, so you can choose to look elsewhere if that's not what you want. Actually, he does connect things to spiritual development a bit--religiously, he's a Vajrayana Buddhist with a central Dzogchen practice, and he does give some pointers for how to connect the practices he teaches to spiritual development if you happen to follow the same spiritual path that he does, but it's definitely optional and not a major focus of his work.

replies.title: Re: Someone teaching a magical course for $100 a month for a course of seven months
replies.content:
I've had no personal contact with Jason Miller and haven't read his work -- he's very nearly as far as you can get from the kind of magic I do and still be an occultist. As a general rule, though, anybody who charges that kind of money for magical instruction will profit from it much more than their students will. (Though it can be a useful initiation to get a good personal reminder of that old adage about a fool and his money!)

The real secrets of magic can't be bought for money. The only thing that will buy them for you is the currency of your own hard work, self-mastery, and self-knowledge.

comments.title: Petroglyphs & Oliver Anthony
comments.content:
I’ve got a couple of unrelated questions for you this week, JMG.

The first has to do with residual spiritual energy. Not far from where I live is one of the most extensive set of petroglyphs on the continent. It is the record of visions over the course of countless centuries. Kept secret by the local First Nation, white folks ‘discovered’ it in 1954 and the government created a park around it and charged admission fees. Of course, the medicine people of the local First Nation were not consulted. Called ‘the teaching stones’ in the Ojibwe language, the site is considered to be a living entity and according to oral tradition some carvings are over 6,000 years old. Anyway, long story short, I have been there a few times in the past and have felt great peace and elation there. A couple weeks ago, I went again – this was the first time since starting the Modern Order of Essenes course. This time my experience was different: almost immediately after entering the ‘sanctuary’ I felt tingling all over my body and especially in my hands. Putting out my hands towards the dome-like rock was like in a ‘tingling’ equivalent of holding one’s hands out to a blazing campfire. I was not so much surprised by the experience but by the intensity of it. I should add that in recent years the petroglyphs are visited by members of First Nations from all over to say prayers and perform ceremonies and the local First Nation accommodates them any time, any day. It is also the site of daily rituals by the local Curve Lake First Nation and is bathed by water once a week via a copper pot (which is considered a ‘spiritual metal’ by the First Nation). Anyway, my question is about residual energy from spiritual places: for how long is it detectable to spiritual sensitives? (I’m thinking, for example, of your direct experience with ancient holy places in Britain).

The second has to do with the recent musical/social phenomenon of ‘Rich Men North of Richmond’ by Oliver Anthony, but from a spiritual/occult perspective. (Of course, this topic is so rich in occult potential that it could easily fill a decent-length post by a knowledgeable occultist [hint, hint!]) Clearly the song has extremely potent magic (not deliberate, of course, given the background of the composer-performer). But one thing that I find interesting is that the name ‘Oliver Anthony’ is actually the grandfather of the artist (whose real name is Chris Anthony Tunsford) who grew up in dirt-poor-but-proud-and-resilient Appalachia of the 1930s. Besides being a touching tribute, I wonder if (and, if so, to what extent) this homage to the ancestors has helped to put ‘wind under the wings’ of the hit song. I am reminded of the traditional Native American saying that ‘those who honour the ancestors are, in turn, honoured by the ancestors’. Your thoughts on this specific point?

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Petroglyphs & Oliver Anthony
replies.content:
1) Well, to begin with, you're not dealing with residual spiritual energy -- that holy place is being tended regularly in a sacred manner, so it's not surprising that it's buzzing! Even a neglected place will keep power for some time, however -- the period depends on what kind of workings were done there, and also what material substances are involved. Rock holds magical energies longer than just about anything else, which is part of why many stone circles in Britain are still buzzing with power more than three thousand years after the collapse of the society that created them.

2) Good question. That may be a factor, but it's not a subject I've studied closely -- not least because my own Scots Presbyterian ancestors would be horrified by what I do!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Cabala (spelled however) was originally Pagan. As Gershom Scholem showed in his magisterial history The Origins of the Kabbalah, it wasn't originally Jewish at all -- Jewish mystics borrowed it from the Gnostics. The Gnostics, in turn, got it from Neo-Pythagorean schools more or less affiliated with Middle Platonism, which was wholly Pagan. That's why so many of the core words (for example, sephirah and gematria) aren't Hebrew -- they're Hebraic pronunciations of Greek words: sphaera, "sphere," and grammateia (from grammata, "letters"). Jewish people in the ancient and early medieval worlds weren't isolated groups cut off from the intellectual life of the time -- they were full participants in the spiritual scene, and influenced and were influenced by many other traditions.

There have been a couple of books on Cabala for Pagans, but I don't know if most of them are still in print -- they're also mostly focused on Wicca, which may or may not be your cup of tea. My book The Celtic Golden Dawn has some material on Cabala from a Celtic polytheist standpoint, if that's of interest.

comments.title: Druidry or Celtic Golden Dawn?
comments.content:
Hi John,
I am newly interested in Druidry and magick, and am stuck on whether to pursue the Druid path (as laid out by the AODA for example) or the Celtic Golden Dawn path. I am most interested in Druidry, but not far behind that interested in magical self-cultivation. Would you describe some of the key differences between the Druid path and the Celtic Golden Dawn Path?
Thank you

replies.title: Re: Druidry or Celtic Golden Dawn?
replies.content:
Sure. Druidry is a spiritual path -- for some people, a religious path. Meditation, seasonal ritual, and living in harmony with the living Earth are its important practices. While you can take up magic on that path, magic is optional in Druidry and many people don't get into it.

The Celtic GD is a magical tradition, full stop, end of sentence. Magic is what it's all about, and daily practice of magical rituals, along with meditation and divination, is central to its path. It shares the Druid focus on living in harmony with nature, but that's secondary to the work of magical training and self-transformation.

replies.title: Re: Druidry or Celtic Golden Dawn?
replies.content:
A follow up to anon's question: What book/organization/practice you would suggest for the CGD mage to further pursue the spiritual path of druidry? Would it be appropriate to use the Druidry handbook for seasonal practices and devotional work (replacing the SOP with LRP)? Is there another book of that would be better as far as seasonal ritual goes? I am curious for both personal seasonal ritual and for group ritual.

I remember that the AODA requires the SOP and so is not compatible; I've not seen the work and materials of the OBOD, would it be compatible?

I appreciate your advisement.

replies.title: Re: Druidry or Celtic Golden Dawn?
replies.content: You could use the Druidry Handbook, though not the Druid Magic Handbook. You could also definitely use the OBOD system -- in its original form, it included the LBRP.

replies.title: Re: Druidry or Celtic Golden Dawn?
replies.content:

I did some digging as well - I note that your Mysteries of Merlin book is seasonal practices. The CGD website recommends it, so I assume it is also compatible.

Are they purely solitary, and would they complement the CGD at any grade or better left till after completion?
Thank you again.

replies.title: Re: Druidry or Celtic Golden Dawn?
replies.content: The Mysteries of Merlin is a set of eight initiatory rituals done over the course of the year. They're designed for solitary practice, they're spiritual in their focus though they use magical methods, and they are entirely compatible with the CGD work. You can start them alongside the CGD book.

comments.title: Alchemy question
comments.content: Can a cast-iron previously used for cooking, if thoroughly scoured of prior matter (and potentially banished, or cleansed with salt and sunlight, in addition to soap and water), be used in a spagyric working? Or does the potential for small amounts of matter from cooking and the like interfere too much in the preparation?

replies.title: Re: Alchemy question
replies.content: You can cleanse it for alchemical use, but it takes more than scouring. You need to heat it until all the organic material in the pores is reduced to carbon. If you have an electric stove, put it on the burner, turn the burner to high, and leave it there dry until it stops emitting smoke. (It may be dull red by then!) Turn the burner off and leave it there to get completely cool, then wash it thoroughly and dry it. It'll be fine for alchemical use thereafter.

Right now it's been in my head, particularly my forehead and the top of my head, for the last 2-3 months, and it's so intense sometimes I feel delirious; on most days I can barely think (typing this is a bit challenging). I am unable to read much because the energy in my head becomes overwhelming and I feel extremely fatigued after. I had to stop drinking because it gives me a bad headache, my body feels very sensitive right now. When it is particularly intense, I just stay in bed. It has also made some personality changes (some bad traits seem to have been expunged).

I am not sure how much longer this takes, since I have a lot of mental work to do soon, but it seems atleast like hopefully its not too far way.

jmg, some questions for you and others on this topic:

6) You'd said you were probably on your final or final few lifetimes. If someone has such an experience, how far would they be generally speaking according to sources? replies.title: Re: Q

2) Those are two ends of a very, very wide spectrum, and the far end of it -- the end at which the physical body literally dissolves in light and rebirth is at an end -- normally happens only to people who already were functioning at an extremely high spiritual level: saints, for example. 6) It really varies. In my case, I was fairly close to finishing with incarnation a long time ago, then screwed up badly and had to go back through another long round of lives before getting back to where I was. That's not a common situation and it has odd effects. Don't worry about how close you are -- just keep on with your spiritual practices and live a life in harmony with your ideals, and you'll be fine.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In the hydraulic analogy for electrical systems, resistance is how constricted a pipe is. You can shrink a pipe and bump up the pressure difference across the two ends of the pipe, and you still get the same water flow. Similarly, you can double the resistance on a connection and double the voltage running across it, and you still get the same electrical current flow.

Etheric phenomena seem to be affected by the states and transitions of electrons (which includes most of chemistry), for whatever reason, but that has almost no particular relationship to resistance. I expect that the overall energy that goes from electrical processes into electronic states and from there into etheric phenomena, rather than into disordered heat, is tiny (because otherwise that would measurable enough to be a consistently replicable positive finding in paraphysics, and I have yet to see any memos or anything about how would be now allowed to actually start having those). Electrical resistance does generally entail the dissipation of energy, but you could probably get etheric effects with a tiny amount of resistance, or get no coherent etheric effects from a large amount of resistive dissipation, or your etheric effects could be coming from radiative losses to antenna effects or who knows what.

I wonder if candle substitutes that were small enclosed arc lamps would be better or just worse. I mean, worse besides just the battery life problem of course. Or what if they were extremely compact fluorescent lights?

comments.title: Magical Languages

Hello JMG
I am half-way through your guest talk on The Embrace and Expand podcast. Always wonderful to hear the voice behind the genius ! And I was really taken by your comments on the highly sophisticated sound technology of ancient cultures.

When I first began my Yoga studies, I was fortunate enough to meet a teacher of Vedic chanting who had studied directly with T.K.V. Desikachar in India. (A Yoga master himself, Desikachar was also the son of “the father of modern Yoga” Sri Krishnamacharya.)

I have studied Vedic chanting with her (my teacher) for about 14 years now, and for me, TSW.
There are mantras for protection, mantras for all sorts of relationships and mantras for personal well-being. We were taught early on that these mystical and sacred chants were performed in exacting rituals, where, if the fire sacrifices were not done accurately, the entire fabric of the world could be in jeopardy. That is to say, these ancient, sacrificial rituals and chants “upheld “ the structure of the cosmos!

Pretty heady stuff indeed😉

Ok my question: the Sanskrit language is said to be “polished”, “perfected “, “well-done”. There are claims that its sonic dimensions are so powerful as to elicit the form that is suggested by the word itself.

Personally, I’ve come to love a beautiful chant from one of the oldest Upanishads that summons up (to me) the creation of the Universe as described in the Cosmic Doctrine!

Do you think magical evocations and incantations in the English language are as powerful (yet) as are those in other “polished” languages like Hebrew and Latin?
I’m not putting down the English language, or the beautiful songs that come from it, but any deep advantage, in your opinion, for the study of Hebrew, Latin or Greek etc.?

Thank you for all you do.

Jill C

replies.title: Re: Magical Languages
replies.content: It's a curious fact that chanting something in a language you don't use for everyday purposes is always more effective than using your everyday language. It doesn't matter what the language is -- just that you don't use it for ordinary purposes. So learning enough of another language to use it in magic or practical spirituality is a good idea, no matter what the other language is.
